coredata.dc:description: Multimodal imaging is a potent technique that integrates complementary information from various imaging modalities, offering comprehensive insights into disease diagnosis. However, the development of biocompatible multimodal probes remains a challenge. In this study, we present the synthesis, characterization, and application of gadolinium and ytterbium co-doped carbon dots (Yb/Gd-CDs) for triple-modal fluorescence (FL)/magnetic resonance (MR)/computed tomography (CT) imaging. Notably, Yb and Gd were successfully incorporated into the lattice of CDs using a facile one‐pot hydrothermal approach to obtain Yb/Gd-CDs. The Yb/Gd-CDs demonstrated an ultra-small size, excellent colloidal stability, and low toxicity, making them suitable for further in vivo applications. Moreover, the Yb/Gd-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence, high X-ray attenuation (40.17 HU/g/L), and high T1 relaxivity (11.16 mM-1s−1) with an ultra-low r2/r1 ratio of 1.08, showcasing remarkable performance in in vivo fluorescence, T1 -weighted MR, and CT imaging of mice. Yb/Gd-CDs show great promise for in vivo multimodal imaging, and this work provides a highly biocompatible CDs-based theranostic platform with multiple functions for disease diagnosis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and Characterization of Yb/Gd-CDs. TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of Yb/Gd-CDs. Insert in (a) is the particle size distribution of Yb/Gd-CDs as determined from TEM images (n = 200). (c) Hydrodynamic size of Yb/Gd-CDs. (d) EDX spectra of Yb/Gd-CDs and the Yb/Gd atomic percentage were determined at four random probe positions. (e) SEM images of Yb/Gd-CDs and corresponding elemental mapping images of C, N, O, Yb, and Gd. (f) FT-IR spectra of CDs and Yb/Gd-CDs. (g) Raman spectra of Yb/Gd-CDs. (h) Survey XPS spectra of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs. XPS spectra of (i) C 1 s, (j) N 1 s, (k) Yb 4d and (l) Gd 4d in Yb/Gd-CDs. Black line: Raw data. Red line: Fitting data. Blue line: Base line. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Biocompatibility evaluation. (a) Cell viabilities of Yb/Gd-CDs on 3T3 normal cells. (n = 6 independent samples). (b) Blood biochemistry of Yb/Gd-CDs treated mice (0.067 mmol kg−1) at different sacrificed days (control, 1, 14, and 30 days) (n = 3 independent samples). Bio-distribution measurements of the Gd (c) and Yb (d) content in mice organs (n = 3 independent samples). (e) H&E staining slices of corresponding major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) in (b). scale bar: 50 µm. (f) H&E staining slices of the stomach, small intestine, and large intestine of mice orally injected with Yb/Gd-CDs at different sacrificed days (control, 1, 14, and 30 days). scale bar: 50 µm. All above data were presented as means ± standard deviation (SD).

Fluorescence properties and FL imaging of Yb/Gd-CDs. UV–Vis-NIR absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a) and Yb/Gd-CDs (b) with different excitation wavelengths. (c) The confocal fluorescent microscopic images of 4T1 cells incubated with PBS and Yb/Gd-CDs. (d) Fluorescence images of PBS and Yb/Gd-CDs with different excitation wavelengths and emission wavelengths, recorded by a CCD camera of an in vivo imaging system. (e) In vivo fluorescence imaging of nude mice and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) after intravenous injection of PBS and Yb/Gd-CDs (excitation: 430 nm; emission: 500 nm).

In vitro and in vivo CT imaging. (a) In vitro CT images and (b) CT values versus Yb/Gd-CDs NPs (red line) concentrations and iohexol (black line) concentrations (at 80 kV). (c) In vivo CT imaging of upper GI tract at other time points after oral administration of Yb/Gd-CDs. (d) In vivo CT imaging of Kunming mice after intravenous injection of Yb/Gd-CDs. CT values of kidney (e) and liver (f) region after intravenous injection of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs at different time points. The data were presented as means ± standard deviation (SD) (n = 3 independent samples). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vitro and in vivo MR imaging. Longitudinal relaxivities (a) and T1 -weighted MR imaging images (b) of Yb/Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA at a series of Gd concentrations. (c) In vivo T1 -weighted MR imaging. T1 MR signals of kidney (d) and liver (e) after intravenous injection of Yb/Gd-CDs at different time points. (n = 3 independent samples). (f) T1 -weighted imaging of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice after intravenous injection of Yb/Gd-CDs. (g) T1 signal intensity of tumor region after intravenous injection of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs at different time points. (n = 3 independent samples). All The data were presented as means ± standard deviation (SD).
Schematic illustration of biocompatible Yb/Gd-CDs synthesis for in vivo multimodal imaging.

head.ce:title: Gadolinium and Ytterbium-Doped carbon dots for Fluorescence/Magnetic Resonance/Computed tomography multimodal imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A biocompatible gadolinium and ytterbium-doped CDs (Yb/Gd-CDs) was synthesized for in vivo CT/MR/FL trimodal imaging. The Yb/Gd-CDs provided excellent solubility, colloid stability, ultra-small size, and remarkable biocompatibility. Besides, Yb/Gd-CDs provided excellent fluorescent properties, high X-ray attenuation (40.17 HU/g/L) and notable T1 relaxivity (11.16 mM-1s−1) with ultra-low r2 /r1 ratio of 1.08, conferring distinct intrinsic contrast in vivo. Moreover, comprehensive toxicity assessments and biodistribution analyses established Yb/Gd-CDs as a practical, safe agent for bioimaging applications. These facial synthesized Yb/Gd-CDs displayed significant advantages, pioneering a new approach for high biocompatible multimodal imaging probes.

Multimodal imaging is a potent technique that integrates complementary information from various imaging modalities, offering comprehensive insights into disease diagnosis. However, the development of biocompatible multimodal probes remains a challenge. In this study, we present the synthesis, characterization, and application of gadolinium and ytterbium co-doped carbon dots (Yb/Gd-CDs) for triple-modal fluorescence (FL)/magnetic resonance (MR)/computed tomography (CT) imaging. Notably, Yb and Gd were successfully incorporated into the lattice of CDs using a facile one‐pot hydrothermal approach to obtain Yb/Gd-CDs. The Yb/Gd-CDs demonstrated an ultra-small size, excellent colloidal stability, and low toxicity, making them suitable for further in vivo applications. Moreover, the Yb/Gd-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence, high X-ray attenuation (40.17 HU/g/L), and high T1 relaxivity (11.16 mM-1s−1) with an ultra-low r2/r1 ratio of 1.08, showcasing remarkable performance in in vivo fluorescence, T1 -weighted MR, and CT imaging of mice. Yb/Gd-CDs show great promise for in vivo multimodal imaging, and this work provides a highly biocompatible CDs-based theranostic platform with multiple functions for disease diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Medical imaging is vital in diagnosing, staging, prognosis, and treating various diseases. Medical imaging technologies, including computed tomography (CT) [1–3], magnetic resonance (MR) imaging [4–9], fluorescence (FL) imaging [10–12], and single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) [13,14], have attracted tremendous interest. CT and MR imaging provide noninvasive and comprehensive anatomical information with high spatial resolution. FL and SPECT technologies, with high sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), enable the acquisition of more detailed lesion information at the molecular and cellular levels. Contrast agents (CAs) are necessary when certain lesions, such as tumors and inflammations, are not distinguishable from normal structures. CAs can accumulate in target organs or lesions through blood circulation via active or passive targeting, thereby enhancing the signal at regions of interest (ROI) for disease diagnosis. However, contrast agents like Gd-DTPA and iohexol have shorter half-life times, resulting in a limited imaging time window [15–17]. Each imaging mode offers limited anatomical and lesion information. Nanoparticle-based contrast agents (CAs) with stable and excellent properties can overcome these limitations by integrating multiple imaging modalities into a single nanostructure. However, current synthetic methods for multimodal nanoparticle-based CAs involve complicated procedures, are time-consuming, entail complex composition and size control, and exhibit poor water solubility [18–20]. Importantly, multiple modal nanoparticle-based CAs contain independent imaging moieties, leading to unpredictable toxicity due to complex metabolic and excretion pathways [21,22]. Therefore, developing a multimodal nanosized imaging probe with a simple synthesis process and excellent biocompatibility is crucial.

In this work, we proposed a facile one-pot hydrothermal strategy to obtain biocompatible gadolinium and ytterbium-doped CDs (Yb/Gd-CDs) for in vivo CT/MR/FL trimodal imaging (Scheme 1 ). Citric acid and urea were selected as carbon sources, and Gd3+ and Yb3+ were introduced to dope in the carbon skeleton of CDs. As-synthesized Yb/Gd-CDs provided excellent solubility, colloid stability, ultra-small size, and remarkable biocompatibility. Yb/Gd-CDs provided excellent fluorescent properties with multiple color emissions for cell imaging and in vivo bioimaging. Besides, Yb/Gd-CDs possessed high X-ray attenuation (40.17 HU/g L-1) and notable T1 relaxivity (11.16 mM-1s−1) with ultra-low r2 /r1 ratio of 1.08, conferring distinct intrinsic contrast in vivo. Systematic CT and MR imaging experiments demonstrated the immense potential of Yb/Gd-CDs in biological imaging. Moreover, comprehensive toxicity assessments and biodistribution analyses established Yb/Gd-CDs as a practical, safe agent for bioimaging applications. These facial synthesized Yb/Gd-CDs displayed significant advantages, pioneering a new approach for high biocompatible multimodal imaging probes.

All materials used were at least analytically pure. Gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate (Gd(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.99 %), ytterbium nitrate pentahydrate (Yb(NO3)3·5H2O, 99.99 %), citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), urea (99 %) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Concord Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) medium 1640 basic was purchased from GIBCO Co. (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Lanzhou Bailing Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Lanzhou, China). Ultrapure water was purchased from Hangzhou Wahaha Co., Ltd. Penicillin–streptomycin solution was bought from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on a Philips Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (Philips, Eindhoven, Netherlands) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV). Elemental and SEM-energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analyses were performed on a Zeiss Gemini SEM 500 microscopy (Carl Zeiss AG, Oberkochen, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained by a Rigaku D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). A Nicolet IS 10 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Madison, USA) with KBr plates was used to record the FT-IR spectra (4000–400 cm−1). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained by a K-alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). The contents of Gd and Yb elements in the as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs were determined by inductively coupled plasma–mass spectrometry (ICP-AES) (IRIS Advantage, Thermo, USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was recorded on a Malvern Zetasizer (Nano series ZS 90, UK). The fluorescence measurements were performed on an F-4500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra were measured on a UV-3600 plus spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

Yb/Gd-CDs were synthesized through a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach. Briefly, 27 mg Gd(NO3)3·6H2O, 108 mg Yb(NO3)3·5H2O, 210 mg citric acid, and 780 mg urea were successively added into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by the addition of 5 mL ultrapure water. After ultrasonically dissolving the mixture, it was heated to 160 °C and reacted for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the products were precipitated by adding 40 mL of ethanol. The precipitate was washed several times with 1 mL ultrapure water and 8 mL methanol. After drying in the vacuum oven, the as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs were obtained for further use (yield of Yb/Gd-CDs: 12.18 %; yield of CDs: 10.81 %).

To assess the long-term colloid stability, Yb/Gd-CDs was dispersed in various media (water, PBS, normal saline, FBS, and RPMI-1640 culture medium) at 37 °C and their photos were recorded. Besides, to assess the chemical stability of Yb/Gd-CDs. Yb/Gd-CDs solution was dialyzed for 3 days with dialysis bag and the outer solution was collected and incubated with the Xylenol orange (XO) indicator (0.2 %).

Murine breast cancer cell lines (4T1 cells) and mouse embryonal fibroblast cells (3T3 cells) were obtained from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and cultured with RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin, then placed in an incubator filled with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

A standard MTT assay was used for determining the in vitro cytotoxicity of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs. Briefly, 3T3 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. Then, the culture medium was removed and washed with PBS three times. After adding a new culture medium, different contents of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs were added into the 96-well plate to incubate for another 24 h (final concentrations of Yb/Gd-CDs were 0, 50, 80, 100, 200, 400, 600 and 800 µg mL−1, respectively). After the 96-well plate was washed with PBS, 10 µL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to incubate for another 4 h. Then, 120 µL of DMSO was added into each well to dissolve the MTT-formazan, and the absorbance at 490 nm was determined to evaluate the cell viability.

Kunming mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 4) with different treatments: Group 1: No treatment; Group 2, group 3, and group 4 were intravenously administered with 150 µL of Yb/Gd-CDs (50 mg mL−1) and sacrificed at 1, 14 and 30 days, respectively. The body weights of the control group (group 1) and experiment group (group 4) were recorded daily. The blood samples were collected from the mice, and the biochemical analyses were measured. In addition, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) were extracted and used for H&E staining for histological analysis. ICP-AES analyzed each organ's Gd and Yb levels at different time points.

Female Balb/c mice, Kunming mice, and Balb/c nude mice (25 g) were obtained from Beijing HFK Bioscience Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All animal studies were carried out under the protocol approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the second hospital of Tianjin Medical University (YK2022K147). The 4T1-tumor models were established by subcutaneous injection of 1 × 106 4T1 cells. The 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were used for further experiments when the tumor size reached 0.8 cm3.

4 T1 cells (1 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in a 6-well plate in an incubator filled with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. After incubating for 24 h, the cells were treated with Yb/Gd-CDs (150 µg mL−1) for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). With excitation wavelengths at 420, 460, and 545 nm, CLSM images were recorded in the blue, green, and red channels. Bright-field images were also captured for confirmation of the cell location.

The quantum yield (QY) of Yb/Gd-CDs was determined using a quinine sulfate solution (in 0.1 M H2SO4, quantum yield: 54 %) as a standard solution. The QY was calculated according to the following equation:
QY = Q
Y R
I I
R
A R
A
η 2
η R 2

The QYR presents the quantum yield of quinine sulfate solution (54 %). I and IR are the integrated fluorescence intensity of Yb/Gd-CDs and quinine sulfate solution, respectively. A and AR are the absorbance of Yb/Gd-CDs and quinine sulfate solution at 350 nm, respectively. The η stands for the refractive index (η water = 1.33).

The Balb/C nude mice were intravenously injected with 200 µL of Yb/Gd-CDs (25 mg mL−1) or PBS (pH 7.4,10 mM). CCD camera in the IVIS imaging system was used to record fluorescence images of mice (excitation: 430 nm; emission: 500 nm). The mice were sacrificed, and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) were extracted for fluorescence imaging.

The Yb/Gd-CDs and clinical iohexol were dispersed in PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) with different concentrations (0, 5, 15, 30, 40, and 50 mM Yb or I) to assess the CT contrast ability. These tubes were scanned in a GE HDCT750 imaging system according to the following parameters: FOV 256 × 256 mm; slice thickness 0.6 mm; tube voltage 80 kV; tube current 210 mA.

For in vivo metabolic distribution imaging, 150 µL of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs (45 mg mL−1) was intravenously injected into each Kunming mouse. CT images at different time points were acquired to observe the liver and kidneys. For in vivo tumor imaging, 150 µL of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs (50 mg mL−1) were intravenously injected into the 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice. For in situ tumor imaging, 50 µL of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs (50 mg mL−1) were intratumorally injected into the tumor site. The scanning parameters were the same as above mentioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo CT imaging of the upper gastrointestinal (GI) tract
ce:section.ce:para: For upper GI tract imaging, 400 µL of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs (40 mg mL−1) were orally administered to Kunming mice. Besides, 400 µL of iohexol solution at the same molar concentration was also administered for contrast. After that, upper GI tract imaging was performed by CT at different time points. The scanning parameters were the same as above mentioned.

To assess the T1 relaxivity (r1 ) and T1 -weighted MR imaging, the Yb/Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA were dispersed in water with different Gd concentrations. Then scanned with a 3.0 T MR imaging system (Siemens GmbH, Berlin, Germany). The scanning parameters were as follows: T1 mapping sequence with TR = 5.0 ms, TE = 1.9 ms, flip angle = 2° and 10°, FOV = 200 × 200 mm, layer thickness = 2.0 mm, and number of slices = 20. The T2 relaxivity (r2 ) of Yb/Gd-CDs was obtained according to the following parameters: TR = 2200 ms, TE = 7.5 ms, echo spacing = 7.5 ms, FOV = 60 × 40 mm, matrix = 128 × 128, slice thickness = 1.5 mm, slice spacing = 0.5 mm.

For MR imaging at a cellular level, 4 T1 cells (1 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in a 6-well plate in an incubator filled with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. After incubating for 24 h, the cells were treated with Yb/Gd-CDs (0, 50, 200, 400 µg mL−1) for 24 h. Then, the cells were treated with trypsin, and the cell precipitations were obtained by centrifugation. These tubes containing cell precipitations were scanned at a 3.0 T MR imaging system.

To assess the in vivo MR imaging ability of Yb/Gd-CDs, the Kunming mice were intravenously injected with Yb/Gd-CDs (150 µL, 45 mg mL−1). At different time intervals, MR images were taken from a 3.0 T MR imaging system to show the structure of the liver and kidneys. The 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with 150 µL of Yb/Gd-CDs (50 mg mL−1) for tumor imaging.

Yb/Gd-CDs were synthesized through a facile one-pot hydrothermal process by mixing the Gd3+, Yb3+, citric acid, and urea precursors and reacting at 160 °C for 4 h. The morphology and size of Yb/Gd-CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The Yb/Gd-CDs showed a uniform distribution and were well-mono-dispersed with an average particle size of 4.32 ± 0.68 nm (n = 200) (Fig. 1
a and 1b). Besides, an obvious lattice structure with 0.25 nm fringe spacing was observed, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite, indicating the formation of CDs [79]. The average hydrodynamic size of Yb/Gd-CDs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was about 13.4 nm with a polydispersity index of 0.195, indicating a good dispersity (Fig. 1 c). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was used to determine the structure of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs ( Figure S1 ). Compared with CDs, the diffraction peaks in Yb/Gd-CDs exhibit reduced intensity and display a distinctive peak at approximately 23°, accompanied by a broad background signal noise, indicating weak crystallinity of Yb/Gd-CDs. The observed weak crystallinity of Yb/Gd-CDs may be attributed to incorporating Yb and Gd atoms into the graphite frameworks of CDs. Quantitative scanning electron microscopy (SEM)-energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis was used to evaluate the elemental composition. The Yb/Gd atomic ratios in four randomly selected probe positions of Yb/Gd-CDs exhibited concordance, suggesting a good distribution of Gd and Yb in the Yb/Gd-CDs (Fig. 1 d). Besides, a collection of Yb/Gd-CDs blocks was used to obtain the overall elemental mapping images. Elemental mapping images demonstrated the good distribution of C, N, O, Gd, and Yb in the Yb/Gd-CDs blocks (Fig. 1 e).

Subsequently, the FT-IR spectra were further used to determine the functional groups in Yb/Gd-CDs (Fig. 1 f). A broad absorption band from 3300-3500 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H. The peaks at 1050 and 1380 cm−1 are ascribed to the C-N and N-H groups, respectively, suggesting a nitrogen-doping in Yb/Gd-CDs. Besides, the 1440 and 1600 cm−1 peaks originated from the C-O and C = O groups, respectively, indicating the existence of a carboxyl group (–COOH). The carboxyl group might be derived from the citric acid facilitating the chelation of Gd or Yb ions to form the double metal ion doped CDs. In addition, the structural characteristics of Yb/Gd-CDs were characterized by Raman spectra. Under the excitation of 638 nm, two obvious peaks were assigned to the D band at 1375 cm−1 and G band at 1560 cm−1, respectively, indicating the Yb/Gd-CDs possessed a similar structure to graphite (Fig. 1 g). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured to evaluate the elements of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs (Fig. 1 h). The full survey XPS spectrum of Yb/Gd-CDs showed five obvious characteristic peaks at 284.4, 401.4, 531.7, 142.9, and 185.6 eV assigning to the C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, Gd 4d, and Yb 4d, and the corresponding atomic percentages were 51.69 %, 6.05 %, 39.9 %, 1.01 %, and 1.26 %, respectively. The XPS curve of C 1 s spectra showed three obvious peaks at 284.6, 285.4, 286, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to C-C/C = C, C-N, C-O, and C = O functional groups, respectively (Fig. 1 i). In the N 1 s spectra, three obvious peaks were observed at 397.7 eV (pyridinic N),399.2 eV (pyrrolic N), and 401.3 eV (graphitic N) indicating an N-doping in Yb/Gd-CDs (Fig. 1 j). Meanwhile, the spectra of O 1 s exhibited two peaks of C = O at 531.2 eV and C-O at 532.4 eV ( Figure S2 ). The XPS curve of Yb displays two obvious peaks at 184.9 eV and 188.3 eV, which are assigned to the binding energy of Yb 4d5/2 and Yb 4d3/2, respectively (Fig. 1 k). Meanwhile, the Gd displays two peaks at 142.9 eV and 148 eV, which were assigned to the binding energies of Gd 4d5/2 and Gd 4d3/2, respectively (Fig. 1 l). Besides, the Gd and Yb contents in Yb/Gd-CDs were measured by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) at 5.63 % and 26.25 %, respectively. These XPS spectra results demonstrated the C, N, Gd, and Yd-doped properties of the Yb/Gd-CDs.

Considering the significance of the toxicity of nanoprobes in biological applications in living animals, we have investigated the in vitro and in vivo toxicity of Yb/Gd-CDs. The cytotoxicity of as-prepared Yb/Gd-CDs was determined by standard MTT assay (Fig. 2
a). Even at a high concentration of Yb/Gd-CDs, up to 800 mg L-1, the cell viability remained above 80 %. The results indicated no obvious cytotoxicity of Yb/Gd-CDs towards 3T3 cells. The toxicity of Yb/Gd-CDs towards mice was measured by blood biochemistry, body weight monitoring, and histological examination, respectively. During the monitoring, the body weights of the experimental and control groups showed a similar increasing tendency, and no deaths or significant body changes in body weights were observed, suggesting no interference of the Yb/Gd-CDs on the growth of mice ( Figure S5 ). In blood biochemistry, the liver function (alanine aminotransferase, ALT; aspartate aminotransferase, AST; albumin, ALB; total protein, TP; globulin, GLO; total bilirubin, TBIL) and kidney function (creatinine, CRE; uric acid, UA; blood urea nitrogen, BUN) of the mice at 0, 1, 14 and 30 days were measured (Fig. 2 b). The results indicated that all the experimental group's parameters appear normal compared with the control group, suggesting no liver or kidney damage was induced by the administration of Yb/Gd-CDs. In addition, the Gd and Yb content were measured by ICP-AES. The Gd and Yb content reached a summit at 1 day, especially in the liver and spleen region, which may contribute to the uptake of Yb/Gd-CDs by the macrophages in the reticuloendothelial system (RES) organs such as spleen and liver. As expected, the Gd and Yb levels performed a sustained decrease over time, and all the organs returned to the initial after 30 days post-injection (Fig. 2 c and 2d). Besides, the major organs (intravenously administrated: heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys; orally administrated: Stomach, small intestines, and large intestines) were stained with H&E for the histological examination. No obvious lesions and inflammation were observed in the slices of all the organs (Fig. 2 e and 2f). The results suggested that the Yb/Gd-CDs had no notable toxicity to hepatic and kidney function. In view of the toxicity assay above, the Yb/Gd-CDs showed good biocompatibility and can be used for further multimodal imaging in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and FL imaging of Yb/Gd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of as-synthesized Yb/Gd-CDs were determined by the ultraviolet–visible-near infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) absorption spectra and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The UV–Vis-NIR absorption spectra of CDs and Yb/Gd-CDs exhibited an obvious peak at 337 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* transitions induced fluorescence (Fig. 3
a and 3b). The Yb/Gd-CDs showed a characteristic wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission owing to the different emissive trap sites on the surface of Yb/Gd-CDs. The emission peak of CDs and Yb/Gd-CDs ranged from 460 nm to 540 nm under wide excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 460 nm. The Yb/Gd-CDs solution displayed a bright blue emission of 460 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Notably, the fluorescence was immensely enhanced after doping with metal ions, suggesting the formation of new emission energy traps by destroying the CD's host structures. Compared with the spectrum of CDs, no obvious wavelength shifts were observed in the fluorescence spectrum of Yb/Gd-CDs. The quantum yield of Yb/Gd-CDs was calculated to 14.2 % as quinine bisulfate regarded as a standard. The outstanding optical properties facilitated in vitro and in vivo bioimaging. Besides, we have further investigated the photostability of Yb/Gd-CDs under different conditions including H2O2, UV lamp radiation time and NaCl [80,81]. After the Yb/Gd-CDs were incubated with 100 mM H2O2 and irradiated with UV lamp for 60 min, the fluorescence intensity showed a small decrease. Even at a high concentration of NaCl (1 M), the fluorescence intensity was retained almost up to 80 % ( Figure S6 ). These results indicated a good photostability of Yb/Gd-CDs.

To verify the potential fluorescence imaging capacity of Yb/Gd-CDs to serve as a fluorescence probe for cell imaging, in vitro cellular uptake experiments were performed on 4T1 cells. The PBS-treated 4T1 cells showed no obvious fluorescent signal under a confocal fluorescent microscope. In contrast, the 4T1 cells treated with Yb/Gd-CDs showed visualized blue, green, and red fluorescence under 420, 460, and 545 nm excitation, respectively (Fig. 3 c). The bright field images were also captured for cell localization. The blue, green, and red fluorescence were consistent with the characteristic wavelength-dependent emission of Yb/Gd-CDs. The strong fluorescence behavior of 4T1 cells may be attributed to the endocytosis of Yb/Gd-CDs. The outstanding capacity for multiple color emission cell imaging laid a good foundation for in vivo fluorescence imaging. Before in vivo fluorescence imaging, the imaging ability of Yb/Gd-CDs in an IVIS imaging system was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 d, it produces the brightest fluorescence emission under the excitation of 430 nm and the emission of 500 nm. The results are consistent with the fluorescence emission spectra in Fig. 3 c.

Encouraged by the remarkable fluorescence performance in vitro and cell imaging, the in vivo fluorescence imaging was performed on a nude mouse. After injection of Yb/Gd-CDs via intramuscular and subcutaneous injection, a clear vision of fluorescence imaging was observed, revealing a remarkable fluorescence imaging ability of Yb/Gd-CDs in vivo ( Figure S7
). Yb/Gd-CDs were also intravenously injected to evaluate the in vivo imaging performance (Fig. 3 e). An obvious fluorescence of the liver region was observed in the mice injected with Yb/Gd-CDs, while the liver region of mice injected with PBS showed no fluorescence signal. The mice were sacrificed, and the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) were imaged (1 h post-injection of Yb/Gd-CDs). The Yb/Gd-CDs treated mice showed strong fluorescence signal in the liver and kidney, and almost no fluorescence was observed in the heart and lungs. In contrast, the mice injected with PBS showed weak fluorescence, ascribed to the tissue-induced auto-fluorescence. The above results exhibited an outstanding fluorescence imaging ability of Yb/Gd-CDs for in vitro and in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo CT imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
The Yb and Gd in Yb/Gd-CDs possessed large atomic numbers, enabling a relatively high X-ray attenuation coefficient for CT imaging. The CT imaging ability of Yb/Gd-CDs was explored with iohexol (a clinically commonly used CT contrast agent) as a comparison. The CT images of Yb/Gd-CDs and iohexol displayed a similar concentration-dependent brightening effect at 80 kV (Fig. 4
a). In addition, the Hounsfield units (HU) value linearly increased with the Yb and I concentration increase. At the same number of Yb and I, the HU value of Yb/Gd-CDs was higher than the clinical iohexol, suggesting a more remarkable CT contrast ability than iohexol (Fig. 4 b). The X-ray attenuation of Yb/Gd-CDs was calculated to be 40.17 HU/g L-1.

The potential imaging ability of Yb/Gd-CDs in the upper gastrointestinal (GI) tract was explored by conducting a structure display. The 3D renderings of CT images clearly showed the GI structure after oral administration of Yb/Gd-CDs. After 1 minute (min) of oral administration, the major digestive organs, including the stomach and duodenum, began to be bright (Fig. 4 c). In particular, the CT value of the stomach and duodenum increased remarkably. The small intestine’s structure appeared bright after 30 min, revealing a remarkable contrast effect of Yb/Gd-CDs. It is ascribed to the digestion behavior and more accumulation of Yb/Gd-CDs in this region. After 2 h, the large intestines began to fill with Yb/Gd-CDs, and the colons began to be bright at 6 h. Almost all the Yb/Gd-CDs have been excreted from the mice after 1 day. The clear description of the intestinal loops facilitated the diagnosis of digestive diseases such as inflammation, tumor, and external-force lesions, demonstrating a good capacity of Yb/Gd-CDs in the in vivo upper GI tract CT imaging.

After intravenously injection of Yb/Gd-CDs, the CT images of Kunming mice were captured at selected time points to observe the metabolic distribution intuitively. Before injection, the CT values of the kidneys and liver were 45 and 65 HU, respectively. After 30 min post-injection, the CT values of the kidneys and liver region began to increase, which showed a brightening effect in the CT images (Fig. 4 d). The signals reached the summit at 1 h post-injection, with the CT values of the kidneys and liver were 58 and 90 HU, respectively (Fig. 4 e and 4f). The CT values of the kidneys and liver region recovered 24 h post-injection, indicating a clearance from the kidneys and liver. Besides, the clinical iohexol was also used for the CT imaging as a contrast ( Figure S8 ). There’s no brightening effect in the liver region, and the kidneys became bright in a few minutes, revealing a quick metabolic behavior with a short circulation time of iohexol. Thus, the clear display of the kidney and liver is beneficial to the diagnosis of diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo MR imaging
ce:section.ce:para:
Yb/Gd-CDs enjoy the ability of T1 -weighted MR imaging due to the existence of the paramagnetic metal element of Gd. The T1 relaxivity (r1 ) of the Yb/Gd-CDs (11.16 mM-1s−1) was 2.79-fold higher than that of the clinical Gd-DTPA contrast agent (4.00 mM-1s−1) under 3.0 T (Fig. 5
a). Besides, the high r1 value was also much higher than other reported Gd-doped CDs [76,82–85]. Notably, the T2 relaxivity of Yb/Gd-CDs was calculated to 12.04 mM-1s−1 ( Figure S11 ) and the ultra-low r2 /r1 ratio (1.08) of Yb/Gd-CDs render it a remarkable T1 -weighted MR imaging contrast agent. Besides, the T1 -weighted MR images of Yb/Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA showed an obvious concentration-dependent brightening effect. The photos of Yb/Gd-CDs were brighter than those of Gd-DTPA, even at a twice lower Gd concentration (Fig. 5 b). After that, we assessed the in vitro imaging ability of 4T1 cells. Similarly, it showed a concentration-dependent brightening effect, and the T1 signals reflected the endocytosis of Yb/Gd-CDs by 4T1 cells ( Figure S12 ). These results confirmed the MR imaging ability of Yb/Gd-CDs, facilitating further in vivo imaging.

Encouraged by the better T1 -weighted MR imaging ability than the clinical Gd-DTPA, the in vivo T1 -weighted MR imaging analysis was explored on Kunming mice. After intravenous injection of Yb/Gd-CDs, T1 -weighted MR imaging images of Kunming mice at different time intervals were obtained to appraise the in vivo metabolism intuitively. An obvious brightening effect was observed in the kidney and liver regions at 10 min post-injection (Fig. 5 c). The T1 signals of kidney and liver regions increased to 4300 and 3000, respectively, with the initial value of 1873 and 1610, respectively. Besides, the kidneys and liver regions reached the maximum accumulation of Yb/Gd-CDs at 10 min. The T1 signals gradually recovered over time and recovered to the initial at 24 h, suggesting an excretion from the kidneys and liver (Fig. 5 d and 5e). Different from the rapid metabolism behavior of Gd-DTPA in clinic, the obvious brightening effect of kidneys and liver regions continued for over 6 h after administration of Yb/Gd-CDs, indicating a long blood-circulation time for better disease diagnosis. Inspired by the ascendant MR imaging capacity of Yb/Gd-CDs, T1 -weighted MR imaging images of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were taken. A visible brightening effect was observed in the tumor region at 15 min post-injection with Yb/Gd-CDs (Fig. 5 f and 5 g). The tumor region was shown as a ring of reinforcement. It may be ascribed to tumors' enhanced permeability and retention effect. The above results proved that the Yb/Gd-CDs nanoprobe possesses high sensitivity in MR imaging.

In this work, we proposed a one-pot hydrothermal process to synthesize biocompatible Yb/Gd-CDs used for in vivo CT/MR/FL trimodal imaging. The Yb/Gd-CDs were 5 nm and showed excellent solubility and stability. Moreover, the in vitro and in vivo biocompatibility evaluation revealed Yb/Gd-CDs as safe CAs for biological applications. The Yb/Gd-CDs showed excellent multiple color fluorescence for cell imaging of 4 T1 tumor cells, allowing a visible fluorescence imaging of tumors. Compared with the reported CDs, The Yb/Gd-CDs enjoyed a high X-ray attenuation capacity (40.17 HU/g L-1) and behaved with a good T1 relaxivity (r1 = 11.16 mM−1 s−1), offering distinct intrinsic contrast in CT and MR imaging in vivo ( Table S1-S3 ). Overall, Yb/Gd-CDs show promise as nanoscale CAs for multimodal in vivo imaging. Beyond the intriguing merits of Yb/Gd-CDs, systematic toxicity assessment and pharmacokinetics must be evaluated for future clinical research. Moreover, we provide a strategy to integrate CDs with functional metal ions or drug molecules, which is attractive to synthesizing theranostic probes for tumor therapeutic or imaging. Given CDs exhibit great merits in many therapeutic applications such as antioxidation, photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy and etc., CDs-based CAs offer a promising imaging-guided theranostic platform for accurate disease diagnosis in the biomedical field in the future.
